P508124.project_abstract: The proposed Program is aimed at providing access to improved irrigation services and safely managed rural Water Supply and Sanitation (WSS) services in selected areas of Armenia through a partnership involving the Government of Armenia (GoA), the World Bank (WB), and Agence Française de Developpement (AFD). Recognizing that the goals of this program cannot be addressed through a single operation, it will be operationalized through a Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA) to facilitate sustainable transformation. The phases are progressive, and each phase maintains a similar structure and builds towards the achievement of the overall program development objective. The structure of the MPA is uninterrupted and overlapping to ensure that the Program's activities continue without a gap in the implementation of the key activities. The MPA includes (i) a six-year Phase 1 (2025-2031) with focused investments in enhancing the institutional capacity of critical water sector entities, revision to the methodology and structure of irrigation and WSS tariffs, completing core water sector strategies and irrigation master plan, finalizing all infrastructure investment feasibility and design stage documents for all proposed investments in irrigation and rural WSS, civil works to modernize five irrigation schemes, and construction/rehabilitation of supply infrastructure in selected settlements for rural water supply on a pilot basis using performance-based conditions (PBCs); and (ii) a six-year Phase 2 (2029-2035) to scale-up and expand infrastructure investments in all remaining irrigation schemes and priority settlements for rural WSS with a focus on operational and financial efficiency, and climate adaptation.  The second phase will place a strong emphasis on institutionalization and widespread uptake of the policies, plans, strategies, and tools developed under the previous phase. The Phase 1 program, using the Investment Project Financing (IPF) lending instrument with PBCs, aims to enhance climate-resilient access, efficiency, and financial sustainability of irrigation and rural WSS in selected areas of Armenia. It will focus on four components: (i) Component 1 – Water Sector Reform and Institutional Strengthening with PBCs: This component will focus on strengthening national water resources policy and planning through development of a comprehensive national water strategy, a ten-year national irrigation strategy, a WSS national strategy, an irrigation master plan, and legal and regulatory reform. It will furthermore improve irrigation sector management and service delivery through a review of tariffs and subsidies, transformation of the Water Supply Agency (WSA) into an efficient and customer-oriented irrigation service provider, strengthening of the Ministry of Territorial Administration and Infrastructure (MTAI) and the Water Committee (WC), and establishing a Irrigation Water Accounting and Adaptation Center (IWAAC) for water and climate monitoring. (ii) Component 2 – Rural Water Supply and Sanitation Enhancement: This component will first focus on institutional strengthening, capacity building, and investment planning of rural WSS services through development of rural WSS sector policies, a roadmap, tariff review, and strengthening management capacities at the national and local levels. Furthermore, it will support investment planning, including the preparation of feasibility studies for priority rural WSS investments for both MPA phases. Lastly, it will support the construction of water supply infrastructure in selected unserved settlements,  which will include rehabilitation of the water network, including household connections, and any necessary water treatment infrastructure. In phase 1, these investments are expected to be in Ararat, Kotayk, Aragatsotn, Shirak, or Tavush, based on technical, economic, socio-political, and institutional considerations (iii) Component 3 – Modernizing Irrigation Infrastructure & System Management: This component will focus on financing rehabilitation and modernization of selected irrigation systems at main, secondary, and tertiary canal levels. Activities include rehabilitation and modernization of headworks, critical sections of main canals, and secondary and tertiary network levels currently managed jointly by WSA at the large main canal and reservoirs level and by Water User Associations (WUAs) at the secondary and tertiary distributary level. It will further focus on rehabilitating some critical infrastructure under the responsibility of the WSA to ensure continuous provision of irrigation water to WUAs in priority command areas. (iv) Component 4 – Project Management: This component will support project implementation, including procurement and financial management (FM) related to implementation of civil works, compliance with agreed environmental and social management measures, and project monitoring and evaluation (M&E). This component will finance staff costs; coordination of the project-financed activities with other ongoing International Financial Institution (IFI) projects in the water sector; and design, implementation, and reporting of baseline and project completion surveys.

P508450.project_abstract: The proposed Project extends the Bank’s continued support to the provision of potable water, sanitation and transport connectivity services in the Pacific region of Colombia. It will complete and operationalize the infrastructure originally envisioned under the Water Supply and Basic Sanitation Infrastructure and Service Delivery Project  (P156239) and the Enhancing Waterway Connectivity and Water Service Provision in Colombia’s Plan PAZcifico Project (P156880). By carrying forward and finalizing these investments, the proposed Project aims to convert partial progress into sustained service delivery outcomes for water, sanitation and transport connectivity in one of Colombia’s most underserved regions, while addressing the bottlenecks that have slowed execution to date. Project implementation will build upon the experience and lessons learned from the FTSP during the execution of the original water and connectivity operations. The MIE, as implementing agency, has established a PIU for project management, fiduciary tasks, reporting, and environmental and social management. Project components include: Component 1: Water supply and sanitation improvement. This component supports expanding and upgrading potable water and sanitation services in urban areas with low coverage. Investments include water intake, treatment, distribution, metering, and sanitation infrastructure to reduce health and environmental risks. It also addresses solid waste management, strengthens the technical, administrative, and operational capacities of the entities responsible for service provision and regulation, and promotes the responsible use and sustainability of water and sanitation systems by the community.Component 2: River and river-maritime transport improvement. This component finances the design, construction, and maintenance of resilient and accessible river and maritime docks, including floating structures. It supports climate-resilient infrastructure, low-emission electric boats, and navigation aids to enhance safety and connectivity. Additionally, it includes capacity building and institutional strengthening, fostering the creation of community-based associations and providing training for transport service providers.Component 3: Project management and environmental and social management. This component provides support for project implementation, including financial management, procurement, disbursement, and the design and execution of environmental and social instruments, as well as operating costs and audits.Component 4: Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC) establishes a mechanism for rapid access to financing in response to eligible crises or emergencies, enabling the reallocation of funds to address major adverse impacts from natural or man-made disasters.

P508698.project_abstract: The proposed Tanzania Sustainable Rural Water Supply and Sanitation Program Phase II (SRWSSP2) aims to increase and sustain access to basic water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) services across rural districts within the United Republic of Tanzania (URT). Building on Phase I, the program addresses persistent WASH service gaps, institutional weaknesses, and climate-related water stress. It will use a hybrid Program-for-Results (PforR) and Investment Project Financing (IPF) approach to enhance governance, incentivize performance, and mobilize private capital through the following three results areas: (i) Increased access and sustainability of at least basic WASH services, (ii) Policy, Institutions and Regulation for strengthened service delivery, and (iii) Maximizing finance for development.The PforR component will target only the mainland of the URT with access to WASH services while the IPF component will provide support to both mainland and Zanzibar. On Tanzania mainline, the IPF will; (i) support strengthening capacity for sustainable water resource management through aquifer mapping, resource planning, dams inventory, and development of digital systems for groundwater and dams monitoring, enhance technical; (ii) Program Implementation through: Support to the Independent Verification Agent (IVA) for results validation, Project management consultancy to strengthen procurement, financial management, M&E, safeguards, and coordination, and updating and dissemination of key safeguards instruments (GRM, Gender Action Plan, Stakeholder Engagement Plan, ESMF, RPF). Priority IPF activities in Zanzibar include: Strengthening of the Water Department through procurement of working tools and software’s for the 2 newly established Divisions (Water Resource Management and Water Supply and Sanitation Management divisions) under MoWEM; Procurement of Groundwater investigation and monitoring equipment; Technical assistance for Developing Feacal Sludge and wastewater management strategy, detailed designs, and tender documents for priority  sanitation investments; Detailed design and tender documents for water schemes development in Pemba; Drafting the National Water and sanitation Act of 2006 and its Regulations; and Rehabilitation works for Zanzibar Water Institute.The operation is aligned with and is part of the AFE Regional WASH MPA, and is expected to yield significant health, economic, and environmental benefits while addressing fiduciary, environmental, and social risks through targeted mitigation measures.

P508930.project_abstract: The first phase of the MPA will establish a Credit Guarantee Vehicle as the first instrument of the Blended Finance Platform to mobilize private capital for investment in critical and resilient infrastructure such as the electricity, transport and logistics, and water and sanitation sector, to create jobs, reduce carbon emissions and deliver infrastructure services for citizens and businesses. Phase 1 of the MPA will consist of three parts namely: Part 1: CGV Capitalization: Provision of the Government Equity Participation to support the capitalization of the CGV and the issuance of CGV Credit Guarantees (including the establishment of the CGV and its management, staffing, governance, operational, monitoring and evaluation, financial management, procurement, environmental and social safeguards, and pipeline development arrangements, systems and procedures), Part 2: Pipeline Development: Provision of support for the development of a pipeline of potential projects for support under the CGV, including client engagement, the carrying out of risk assessments, pre-feasibility and feasibility studies, and legal, financing and operational structuring of projects and Part 3: Project Coordination and Implementation: Provision of support for the PIU and project management, including for financial management, procurement, environmental and social risk management, monitoring and evaluation, stakeholder engagement and coordination, and operational, administrative and compliance support for the establishment, operationalization and launch of the CGV.

P509225.project_abstract: Rationale and Context: Good nutrition is foundational for jobs, empowering individuals with the health and nutrients needed to learn, develop skills, and perform effectively in the workplace. However, Benin continues to face serious nutrition and human capital challenges, with over 30% of children under five suffering from chronic malnutrition and high rates of anemia among children adolescent and women, which continues to impair their physical and cognitive development and hinder future productivity and economic opportunities. These issues are exacerbated by poverty, food price volatility, and limited access to diverse, nutritious foods. Poor feeding practices, low exclusive breastfeeding rates, and high prevalence of infectious diseases further worsen the situation. Gender inequality worsens nutrition challenges, as women—who provide most agricultural labor—have limited access to land and resources, restricting their ability to improve household nutrition. Their lower socio-economic status and long working hours also reduce time for childcare. Governance is a major challenge, as nutrition goals are often overlooked in sectoral plans and budgets due to poor integration and coordination across ministries. Resource allocation is consistently inadequate, with limited specialized staff and weak steering capacity, while funding streams are unpredictable and partner efforts lack effective coordination. Policies remain fragmented, focusing on isolated interventions instead of a comprehensive, multisectoral approach. Climate change further threatens food security, with frequent droughts and floods impacting agricultural yields. Despite national warning systems, agricultural actors remain vulnerable due to inadequate risk management. Other factors include poor access to health services and water, hygiene, and sanitation services as well as improving access to quality and nutritious foods.Investing in nutrition yields high returns, with every US$1 invested in undernutrition expected to generate US$23 in economic benefits, and each dollar spent on the national integrated school feeding program can generate up to 5.2 dollars in GDP returns, especially when linked to local agriculture in partnership with the private sector. Benin has also made early childhood development (ECD) a national priority, focusing on combating malnutrition through multi-sectoral, community-driven approaches and strong government commitment. Addressing these challenges requires strengthening governance, ensuring predictable and sufficient resource allocation, strategic partnerships and empowering vulnerable groups, especially women, to improve food and nutrition security in Benin. Proposed Program Scope and Boundaries:On July 26, 2023, the Government established a National Agency for Food and Nutrition (Agence Nationale de l’Alimentation et la Nutrition, ANAN)  under the Presidency to ensure the implementation of policies, strategies, standards and regulations in all areas of food and nutrition, in accordance with the national policy on food and nutrition. Almost a year later, on July 24, 2024, the Government adopted its new National Policy on Food and Nutrition for 2024-2033, laying out the need for better cross-sectoral approaches and integrated interventions to combat every form of malnutrition and improve food security. The PforR will support a subset of the government’s national nutrition program, focusing on regions with the highest burden of malnutrition based on the National Strategy on Food and Nutrition for 2026-2030. The program boundaries are defined by a set of results areas that align with the government’s strategy and the World Bank’s Country Partnership Framework (CPF, FY27-36) in preparation. The operation will finance a results-based portion of the government’s expenditure program, with Disbursement-Linked Indicators (DLIs) that reflect key milestones in service delivery, coverage, and outcomes.PrDO proposed: To reduce the prevalence of stunting among children under five years of age, improve nutrition outcomes among women of reproductive age, and to strengthen local food systems.PDO of ALAFIA I proposed: To improve utilization of quality integrated nutrition, health, and child development services in targeted areas. Key Results Areas being proposed: •	RA1: Increase utilization of quality integrated nutrition, health, and child development services•	RA2: Enhance community resilience and access to quality nutritious food supply through local engagement and private sector partnershipsAn IPF Component is also being proposed to improve multisectoral coordination, governance, and evidence-based nutrition program design.

P509293.project_abstract: The proposed Green and Future Cities (GFC) Project is a coordinated World Bank Group (WBG) initiative that integrates International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD) and International Finance Corporation (IFC) support to ILBANK and metropolitan municipalities in Turkiye to deliver scalable, sustainable and resilient urban infrastructure and services. The initiative will help participating municipalities address urban development and municipal investment finance challenges by leveraging the international expertise and technical knowledge of IBRD and IFC to provide a broader range of tailored financing instruments. Under the IBRD operation, the Project will deliver a package of investments and technical assistance to the participating metropolitan municipalities to improve access to sustainable and resilient municipal infrastructure and services. The Project will have three components: (i) Component 1. Integrated, multi-sectoral investments in participating municipalities: The component will support municipal investments contributing to more livable, productive, and climate resilient urban infrastructure and services in the participating municipalities. The eligible sector list is expected to include urban transport, and water and sanitation. This component will cover Antalya and Konya Metropolitan Municipalities; (ii) Component 2. Strengthening capacity of ILBANK and municipalities: The component will help ILBANK and eligible municipalities build institutional and technical capacity in municipal finance and sustainable and resilient urban management. (iii) Component 3. Project Management: The component will finance provision of goods, consulting and non-consulting services, operating costs, and training to ILBANK for Project management and implementation support activities.

P509563.project_abstract: The Project aims to improve livelihoods in Aksu district—particularly for vulnerable groups—by expanding sanitation access, creating jobs, and strengthening climate resilience. With 80 percent of the population lacking adequate sanitation, it will help reduce disease and pollution while generating employment through FSM and reuse-related opportunities. Treated sludge will be promoted as fertilizer for non-food crops, cutting reliance on costly chemical inputs and supporting climate adaptation. Community-led efforts will drive health, economic, and environmental improvements, building a more resilient and empowered Aksu community. The Project includes three components:The Project includes three components: Component 1: Developing Sanitation and Fecal Sludge Treatment Infrastructure will finance  the construction of a combined wastewater and fecal sludge treatment plant to enable sludge reuse and by-product generation (Sub-Component 1.1: Combined Wastewater and Fecal Sludge Treatment Plant), and the construction and rehabilitation of on-site sanitation systems for vulnerable households (Sub-component 1.2: On-Site Sanitation Improvements). Component 2: Establishing FSM Services, Local Job Creation and Community Awareness will support creation of FSM units to manage, operate, and maintain sanitation infrastructure and service delivery, a certified vocational training program, and quality assurance and marketing of Sludge By-Products (Sub-component 2.1: Establishment of Sanitation and FSM operators and Vocational Training). It will also support community engagement to raise awareness on FSM and resource recovery benefits (Sub-component 2.2: Community Awareness and Behavior Change Campaign). Component 3: Project Management and Administration, Monitoring and Evaluation, and Knowledge Dissemination will finance overall project implementation, project monitoring and evaluation, and sharing of project learnings and results.

P509605.project_abstract: This Investment Project Financing (IPF) operation will be financed by a small recipient-executed trust fund (RETF) in the amount of US$4.1 million. It would complement the Mali Improving Education Quality and Results for All Project (MIQRA, P164032), and its upcoming second phase. In addition, the project will seek complementarities with other interventions, such as the Mali Community Resilience and Inclusive Services Project – CRISP (P505025), by piloting a model of school construction in locations not covered by other projects. As such, the project proposes to establish a tested model that could be implemented in any of Mali’s diverse geographic areas using other projects, notably MIQRA’s second phase. The project is structured around three key components: Component 1 - Piloting community-based construction models in selected regions; component 2 - Identifying sustainable school construction approaches; and Component 3 - Project Management and Administration, Monitoring and Evaluation, and Knowledge Dissemination. The implementation period is three years.

P509890.project_abstract: The Mozambique Urban Water Security Project aims to increase access to improved water supply services and strengthen service delivery capacity in selected cities by focusing on sector reforms to increase private sector participation; investing in infrastructure to expand water treatment, transport, storage, and distribution capacity in the Maputo metropolitan region and other southern cities; and supporting improved performance of institutions involved in water supply. The project design includes Performance-based conditions to support the performance improvement of regional water utilities, with the aim of modernizing urban water services, improving efficiency, increasing revenues, building resilient infrastructure, and developing skilled water professionals. Ultimately, the project aims to improve public health, economic development, and poverty reduction with a focus on Greater Maputo and the Southern Region, addressing water insecurity and improving performance in key regions as part of the broader country investment to close the gap towards universal access to water and sanitation in Mozambique.

P510227.project_abstract: The Project will adopt an integrated, multisectoral approach focusing on three interrelated topics: (a) the sustainable management of water resources, which is the foundation of water security for people, the economy and biodiversity as water is a shared and finite resource; (b) the water security for agriculture production, the main driver of Parana’s economy, and, at the same time, the reduction of agriculture contribution to water security risks and (c) the water security for potable water supply to increase its resilience to climate risks and to support the universalization goal, while reducing WSS contributions to water security risks. Retroactive financing is being considered for the Project which includes 4 components:Component 1 – Strengthen climate-informed water resources management. By promoting a better integrated, participatory, resilient and data-driven water resources governance and infrastructure, the component contributes to reducing pressures on water resources quantity and quality and supports more effective preparedness and responses to increasing climate change risks. In doing so, it lays the institutional and technical foundations for improving water security across the State, with emphasis on priority areas where activities are implemented in an integrated manner with other components. Subcomponent 1.1 – Modernize water monitoring, information and management instruments.  Subcomponent 1.2 – Improve integrated water resources governance. Subcomponent 1.3 – Reduce urban and peri-urban erosion. Component 2 – Enhance agriculture water security and reduce associated risks. This component is designed to enhance water security for agricultural production amid rising water shortages and land degradation exacerbated by climate change. It also seeks to reduce the risks of water contamination and water shortages in selected urban water sources associated with land degradation, agrochemical use, animal waste and climate change. Efforts will center on strengthening institutional and technical capacities, leveraging partnerships with cooperatives and local organizations, generating innovation and promoting the adoption of sustainable conservation and production practices.Subcomponent 2.1. – Strengthen the State capacity to manage water security risks in agriculture. Subcomponent 2.2 – Increasing Water Security for Family Farming in Areas Affected by Water Deficit and Soil Degradation.Subcomponent 2.3. – Decrease agriculture contribution to water security risks in urban water sources. Component 3 – Reduce water security risk for universal WSS access. This component aims to enhance the climate resilience, quality, and availability of water and sanitation services by reducing reduce water security risks in selected watersheds of urban water sources and addressing both infrastructure and management model gaps in rural areas, in support of the universalization goals. Subcomponent 3.1 – Reduce water security risks in selected urban water sources. Subcomponent 3.2 – Increase access to sustainable rural water and sanitation services. Component 4 – Support Project Management. This component builds the Borrower’s capacity to implement the Project by supporting fiduciary, technical, environmental, social, and monitoring activities. It provides funding for technical assistance, consulting and non-consulting services, training, operating costs, and necessary goods for key government agencies involved in implementation.

P510359.project_abstract: The operation is a multi-phase program approach (MPA) whose development objective is to support participating EAP countries in improving their water security and resilience to climate risks. Phase 1 of the MPA includes the Philippines Accelerated Water and Sanitation Project in Selected Areas (AWSPSA). The project development objectives, results indicators, and activities are aligned with those of the MPA and will contribute to the expected outcomes under the MPA. The proposed Project Development Objective (PDO) is “to increase access to safely managed water supply and sanitation services and improve the performance of water service providers in selected areas of the Philippines.”Component 1. Infrastructure Investments for Safe and Climate Resilient Water Supply Services (US$213.30 million, of which US$197.55 million IBRD).  This Component aims to increase access to safely managed water supply services through infrastructure projects, including construction and/or rehabilitation of complete water supply systems, from source development to household connections.Subcomponent 1a. Water supply infrastructure investments in the Loboc Cluster in Bohol ProvinceThis subcomponent will address the water scarcity and worsening quality that is particularly felt in the Loboc Cluster. Specifically, it will support (a) the development of a bulk water supply system sourced from the Loboc River with a capacity of 60,000 cubic meters/day; (b) the rehabilitation, upgrading, and expansion of existing water distribution systems in the Loboc Cluster; and (c) implementation of watershed protection measures. Subcomponent 1b. Water supply infrastructure investments in Siargao Island in Surigao del Norte ProvinceThis subcomponent will aim to provide safe and reliable water supply for Siargao Island residents and tourists by developing and enhancing the island’s water infrastructure. The focus will be on increasing the efficiencies of current water systems through NRW reduction and developing new sources to meet current and future demand. It will support 58 barangays in eight municipalities. Subcomponent 1c. Water supply infrastructure investments in Jolo and adjacent barangays in two municipalities in Sulu ProvinceThis subcomponent will aim to rehabilitate and upgrade the entire water supply system in the project areas, which are dilapidated and service only 35 percent of the original service area. The objective is to enhance water services' reliability, safety, and resilience to meet current and future water demand. To this end, this subcomponent will support: (a) upgrading, protecting, and treating spring sources to provide 18,000 cubic meters per day of reliable and safe water supply up to 2040; (b) total replacement of the 56-kilometer water distribution network; (c) construction of four new concrete storage tanks; and (d) installation of around 12,378 household connections Component 2. Infrastructure Investments for Safe and Climate Resilient Sanitation and WASH Services in Institutions (US$12.90 million, of which US$12.32 million IBRD). This Component aims to expand access to safe sanitation and inclusive WASH services through investments in sanitation infrastructure such as for fecal sludge management (FSM) and the construction of WASH facilities in schools and public spaces. Subcomponent 2a. Sanitation and WASH in Bohol.This subcomponent will finance the construction of new WASH facilities in 53 public schools, 43 daycare centers, and 69 health centers, some of which also function as evacuation centers during typhoons and other disasters. Related to sanitation, one FSM plant in Bohol is operating but underutilized and another one is planned; their total capacities are sufficient to address the FSM needs of the three most urbanized and populated city/municipalities – Tagbilaran City, Dauis, and Panglao. A TA under Component 4  will be provided to complement this infrastructure. This TA will focus on developing and implementing a regular FSM program requiring massive community and LGU engagement. The TA will also promote proper hygiene practices. Subcomponent 2b. Sanitation and WASH in Siargao, Surigao del NorteThis subcomponent aims to meet the island's FSM needs, given the prevalence of improperly constructed septic tanks and the absence of a sewerage system. The infrastructure and equipment to be constructed will support regular septic tank emptying services throughout Siargao Island. WASH facilities will also be constructed in schools and other institutions.Subcomponent 2c. Sanitation and WASH in Jolo, SuluThis subcomponent will finance the construction of new WASH facilities in 42 public schools, 8 daycare centers, and 3 social welfare centers, some of which also function as evacuation centers during typhoons and other disasters. A TA will also be provided under Component 4 to prepare the municipality for a regular septic tank emptying program and promote proper hygiene behavior and practices.Component 3. Institutional Reforms and Capacity Building (US$13.44 million IBRD). This Component aims to enhance the efficiency and sustainability of WSS services by supporting fundamental institutional reforms and capacity-building of provinces, LGUs, and WSPs. The WSPs will benefit from onsite practical trainings on leadership and governance, technical and commercial operations, financial management, and project management. It also includes project management costs of DILG.Component 4. Project Management and Technical Assistance (US$28.58 million IBRD, of which US$27.58 million IBRD). This Component will support effective project implementation through support to project management and technical assistance, ensuring compliance with World Bank policies while advancing long-term sanitation, water resource management, and financing strategies.Subcomponent 4a Project ManagementThis subcomponent will finance project management costs for the DPWH. Eligible activities will include consulting and non-consulting services, training, and relevant operating expenses required to implement the project following the World Bank policy and guidelines. Subcomponent 4b. Technical Assistance (TA).This subcomponent will finance TA aimed at enhancing sustainable water and sanitation management.  TA under this component will include consulting work that will support, among others, the development of long-term master plans on sanitation and/or sewerage; implementation of pilot sanitation demonstration projects; implementation of an FSM program; conduct of education campaigns on sanitation and hygiene; development and implementation of watershed management programs; comprehensive water resources and energy assessments; and other related work in the project areas. Component 5. Contingent Emergency Response Component (US$0 million). This zero-cost component will allow the GoP to quickly access project funds in response to an eligible disaster, emergency, or crisis. It will enable rapid reallocation of uncommitted project funds to address urgent needs during a natural disaster or crisis (including public health-related emergencies). A declaration of a state of calamity by a qualified national or subnational authority, under applicable laws, could serve as a possible trigger to activate this component.

P510667.project_abstract: The Pakistan Finance for Lowering Emissions in Water Sector (Pak-FLOW) is strategically aligned with Pakistan's Country Partnership Framework (CPF), addressing key objectives across multiple outcomes to foster sustainable development and climate resilience. Specifically, it fulfills the objectives of CPF Outcome 4: Increased Decarbonization, and supported by the World Bank’s Transformative Carbon Asset Facility (TCAF).The Pak-FLOW project will support decarbonization through reducing methane emissions from wastewater treatment processes by converting the biogas to energy, promote energy efficiency measures in water pumping and the supply of clean renewable energy from solar panels. The project incorporates a robust Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification (MRV) system to ensure integrity and continuous improvement of envisaged operations, ensuring its long-term sustainability.
P510667.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P510696.project_abstract: The project aims to support the development of strategic infrastructure and logistics services in the Province of Salta through four main components: (1) Investment in resilient and safe road infrastructure;(2) Development of logistics centers to serve lithium and other productive sector firms;(3) Investment in critical water and wastewater treatment infrastructure for the local communities; (4) Support to the mining sector by strengthening sustainability, oversight, training, and transparency in mining operations.In addition, the project includes a CERC component and a component focused on Project Management.

P511300.project_abstract: The Uganda Cities and Municipalities Infrastructure Development (UCMID) Program aims to enhance the institutional performance of urban local governments and relevant ministries for urban planning and management. It seeks to increase access to climate-smart infrastructure, services, and local economic opportunities, benefiting both urban residents and displaced populations in refugee-hosting areas. The program will address challenges such as rapid urbanization, inadequate transport infrastructure, lack of integrated urban planning, limited technical and financial resources, climate change impacts, and issues in refugee host districts. It aligns with Uganda’s 2025–2035 Country Partnership Framework, building on lessons from the Uganda Support to Municipal Infrastructure Development (USMID) to strengthen urban service delivery. UCMID is a follow-on operation to the USMID and USMID-AF programs aiming to build on the progress and successes under both initiatives. UCMID is urban and nationwide and will be implemented in two Ministries Departments and Agencies (Ministry of Lands, Housing and Urban Development – Coordinating Ministry and Ministry of Local Government), 10 secondary cities (Jinja, Mbarara, Gulu, Arua, Masaka, Hoima, Mbale, Soroti, Lira, Fort Portal), 11 municipalities (Kabale, Moroto, Tororo, Kitgum, Kasese, Kamuli, Mubende, Busia, Ntungamo, Apac, Lugazi, Entebbe) supported under the previous USMID Program, 15 new municipalities (Masindi, Rukungiri, Bushenyi-Ishaka, Mityana, Kumi, Nebbi, Koboko, Kisoro, Kapchorwa, Ibanda, Iganga, Njeru, Bugiri, Sheema, Kotido), and 11 districts hosting refugees (Arua , Isingiro, Kamwenge, Kiryandongo, Lamwo, Maddi-Okollo, Moyo, Obongi, Terego, Yumbe). The program has two main components: Component 1 (PforR) focuses on urban development performance and investments, financing resilient infrastructure and services, and promoting peaceful coexistence in refugee-hosting areas. This component seeks to achieve results in three Result Areas (RAs): (i) RA1: Enhanced local and national level government institutional capacity and performance for sustainable urban management, (ii) RA2: Increased access to climate smart urban infrastructure, services and local economic development, and (iii) RA3: Integrated climate smart infrastructure, services and local economic development for the host communities and refugees.  The IPF component is designed to directly support the achievement of the Program Development Objectives and Disbursement-Linked Indicators (DLIs) by financing upstream activities that enable effective implementation and results delivery under the PforR. It will focus on strengthening national systems for urban development, program coordination, and technical capacity at both central and local government levels. Specifically, the IPF will finance: (i) overall program management specified in the Program Operations Manual (POM), including operational coordination, monitoring and evaluation, ESHS risk management, enforcement and supervision, and the engagement of an Independent Verification Agent (IVA) to conduct the Annual Performance Assessments (APA) and verify results against the DLI matrix; (ii) the development and rollout of key policy, regulatory, and planning tools, such as the urban data platforms, GIS-based systems and digital innovations, and national frameworks for climate-smart and inclusive urban infrastructure; and (iii) technical assistance and targeted capacity building for national and local institutions in areas such as fiduciary systems, land use management, occupational health and safety (OSH), community health and safety, labor and working conditions and climate-resilient planning. The IPF will also support the conceptualization and piloting of a corridor-based approach to urban investment planning. This includes the development of national corridor design guidelines and investment packaging tools that will help structure integrated infrastructure projects—linking roads, drainage, markets, and utility networks—along strategic urban corridors. The IPF component will dedicate US20 million to support ESHS systems improvements and capacity strengthening of key MDAs (MLHUD, MGLSD, NEMA), implementing entities, and Contractors. Activities will include (I) Professionalization and  accreditation of OHS professionals through the Learning Centre on E&S Standards at Makerere University, (II) upgrading and integration of OHS and Labor Management Information Systems to enhance incident management, reporting and compensation, (III) development of socio-economic assessments, (IV) development of  land acquisition protocols and/or resettlement plans— including livelihood restoration; and (V) finance land acquisition and resettlement expenditures; (VI) develop a communication and stakeholder engagement strategy for the Program; and (VII) strengthening  grievance management  systems including worker GRMs.UCMID will also benefit from an Enhanced Implementation Support and Monitoring (EISM) firm hired directly by the World Bank to support the Project Implementation Support Team (PIST) and the World Bank following the enactment of the Anti-Homosexuality Act 2023 in Uganda. The firm will work with NGO/CSOs and country-based development partners since knowledge of the country context and culture are key considerations to be taken onto account. The EISM will: (i) establish an effective and confidential mechanism to receive, manage, refer, and monitor grievances related to discrimination of Sexual and Gender Minorities (SGM). (ii) support outreach and sensitization to Program beneficiaries and communities. (iii) provide capacity strengthening and technical support to the PIST and (iv) regularly monitor and evaluate the implementation of measures to mitigate the risk of discrimination in all WB’s financed operations in Uganda including UCMID.

P511489.project_abstract: This project is a pilot to unlock tourism development at the regional level through multi-sector interventions. The project is designed to address the multifaceted challenges and opportunities for tourism in Arequipa through coordinated multisectoral investments aiming at identified gaps in connectivity, public services and tourism amenities and sites. The project recognizes that effective tourism development requires a holistic approach that integrates infrastructure improvements, cultural heritage preservation, and effective destination management and promotion. The project focuses on developing tourism routes between the city of Arequipa and the Colca Valley (in the Arequipa region) through the following four complementary components:Component 1. Improving the quality of selected tourist attractions : 1.1) Develop and improve services in targeted sites and attractions and 1.2) Rehabilitate cultural sites Component 2. Upgrading roads and investing in water, sanitation and health facilities focusing on : 2.1) Upgrading the quality of key roads along the identified tourism routes, and 2.2) Improving the water and sewage systems in towns on the identified tourist routes 2.3) construction of a health center and forest rehabilitation.Component 3. Institutional strengthening. Component 4. Project implementation.

P511492.project_abstract: With the DJIBOUTI GROUNDWATER RESILIENCE AND WATER SUPPLY PROJECT (P511492) Djibouti will be joining as phase II the MPA Horn of Africa - Groundwater for Resilience Project (P512774), which started with Kenya, Ethiopia, Somalia, and the Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD). This regional project aims to increase sustainable access and management of groundwater resources in the Horn of Africa's borderlands, addressing urgent water supply needs and fostering transboundary cooperation.The project’s development objective is to increase the sustainable access and management of groundwater in rural areas of Djibouti through investments that pair infrastructure with robust operations and maintenance (O&M) arrangements. The project comprises four components: (i) delivering inclusive groundwater services to priority areas via rehabilitation and construction of potable water systems, complemented by fit-for-purpose O&M models (such as operator contracts, service-level agreements, and community-based maintenance with clear accountability and financing mechanisms); (ii) generating groundwater information and strengthening regional and national institutions, including improved hydrogeological mapping, monitoring networks, data systems, and capacity building for policy, regulation, and asset management; (iii) project management, monitoring and evaluation (M&E), knowledge management, and learning; and (iv) a Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC) to enable rapid response to eligible crises and emergencies.Expected outcomes include improved and reliably maintained groundwater access for communities, strengthened regional and national groundwater management institutions capable of overseeing asset management and O&M, professionalized operation and maintenance of rural water supply to reduce downtime and extend asset life, enhanced cooperation among participating countries through IGAD-facilitated platforms and knowledge exchange, and increased resilience to water scarcity driven by climate variability and population growth.
P511492.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P511557.project_abstract: The Project will constitute the Phase 1 of the Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA) ‘Integrated Water Security Program for the Northeast of Brazil’.  Most of the region’s nine states face similar constraints, including limited water availability, recurrent droughts and floods, fragmented institutions, and underdeveloped infrastructure and management tools— further shaped by transboundary watersheds and aquifers, as well as shared hydraulic infrastructure. These commonalities present a valuable opportunity for horizontal learning and collaboration, enabling states to exchange knowledge and build on each other’s innovations and good practices. Several states have already developed effective solutions that are highly relevant and replicable across the region. The MPA will facilitate structured knowledge exchange and coordination across the states, helping reduce negative externalities and enhance the collective impact of water security investments. About 7.3 percent of the Northeast Region’s population lives in the State of Paraiba; within Paraiba, 77.6 are urban residents and 22.4 percent live in rural areas (IBGE, 2022). The state's water security challenges and socioeconomic landscape present a great opportunity to test and refine the Program's strategies. Phase 1 of the MPA comprises activities prioritized by the State Government aimed at improving water security across Paraiba, strengthening key sector water institutions such as the State Water Resources Management Executive Agency (Agencia Executiva de Gestão das Águas, AESA), the State Water and Sanitation Company (Companhia de Água e Esgotos da Paraiba, CAGEPA), and a newly created Executive Management Unit for Rural Sanitation (Gerencia Executiva de Saneamento Rural, GESR), and constructing essential infrastructure such as a wastewater treatment plant (WWTP), the sectorization of the water supply distribution system to reduce non-revenue water (NRW), and the construction of a water transmission main for the Gramame system, all serving the RMJP. The Project builds on lessons from the ongoing Paraiba Improving Water Resources Management and Services Provision Project (Projeto de Seguranca Hidrica or PSH by its acronym in Portuguese),  approved in 2019. PSH includes construction of water mains to transfer water from the São Francisco River’s Integration Project to 27 municipalities in the regions in Agreste and Borborema, automation and partial sectorization of RMJP's water network to reduce NRW, improvements in wastewater collection for energy efficiency, and institutional strengthening of CAGEPA. . The Project will include the following components:Component 1. Foundations for Water Security. This component aims to contribute to proactive WRM in the state by strengthening the state WRM institution (AESA) and consolidating key management instruments related to water quantity, quality, and drought resilience. It will focus on expanding surface and groundwater monitoring, fostering dialogue on the costs and financial sustainability of the water management system, improving water licenses and charges, and establishing water quality standards through a participatory process with society, along with the necessary actions to achieve those standards. The component will also focus on increasing the state’s capacity for drought management through increased monitoring and warning mechanisms for implementation of adequate measures, and the proposition of a state water reuse policy.Component 2. Resilient Water Services. This component is designed to advance the modernization and expansion of WSS, supporting the state water utility (CAGEPA), the state regulatory agency (ARPB), and a recently created unit that will lead the development of a strategy and plan to support rural water supply and sanitation in the state, the Gerencia Executiva de Saneamento Rural (GESR). It also includes the construction and equipment of water infrastructure that will enhance the quality of life for residents, improve environmental outcomes, and promote economic growth in the metropolitan region of Joao Pessoa. It includes the following subcomponents: (2.1) Expansion of the water supply system in RJMP, this subcomponent will fund the construction of a transmission main connected to the Gramame System; the water supply system includes three existing dams, two of them interconnected and classified as large dams, Gramame and Mamuaba dams; (2.2) Reduction of water losses in RJMP, this subcomponent involves constructing and optimizing selected water supply network sectors to reduce physical water losses in the CAGEPA’s distribution network in the RMJP; (2.3) Expansion and modernization of the WWTP in RMJP, this subcomponent comprises civil works and acquisition of equipment to improve and expand wastewater treatment services in the RMJP under a design-and-build contract; (2.4) Support to the Regulatory Agency of Paraiba State, this subcomponent will fund technical assistance and training to strengthen ARPB’s institutional capacity and regulatory effectiveness; and (2.5) Rural Sanitation, which will provide technical assistance to SEIRH for the improvement of management models for the operation and maintenance of WSS services in the state. Component 3. Partnership for Water Security and Project Management. This component will fund knowledge sharing and learning activities between the Borrower and other States in areas of interest for dissemination of best practices including, inter alia, institutional capacity for WRM, data use and information systems, sustainable management models for rural WSS, and NRW control solutions. The component will also provide support to strengthening the technical and administrative capacity of the PMU with respect to technical, financial management, audit, procurement, monitoring and evaluation, social and environmental, and communication and information technology activities, including through the hiring of consultants. Activities will include the provision of independent audit services.

P511719.project_abstract: The project will focus on the irrigation sub-sector. It aims at supporting irrigated agriculture in public irrigation schemes, through: (i) strengthened performance of the operator of the irrigation service; (ii) modernized irrigation infrastructure, reducing losses and ensuring more flexible access to water; and (iii) improved agricultural value addition, productivity and market access.
P511719.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P512709.project_abstract: The proposed operation is the first phase under the Montenegro Water Services and Climate Resilience Multiphase Programmatic Approach (MPA), a two-phase engagement designed to support the long-term transformation of the water sector in Montenegro. The total estimated program cost is approximately EUR 200 million, with a proposed IBRD financing envelope of approximately USD 90 million — USD 35 million for Phase 1 and USD 55 million for Phase 2 - complemented by contributions from other development partners including the OPEC Fund for International Development and, from Phase 2 onwards, EU funds and contributions from other development partners.Phase 1 (late 2026–2031) will focus on priority water supply and wastewater investments in municipalities across Montenegro, emergency and near-term measures to secure coastal water supply, technical assistance to identify long-term solutions for Morača River basin management and Bolje Sestre spring discharge stabilization, and foundational governance and regulatory reforms. Phase 2 (~2029/2030–2035), becoming effective in parallel with the later stages of Phase 1 and triggered by the completion of key analytical and reform milestones and Montenegro's EU accession, will implement the long-term coastal water security solution, scale up water supply and wastewater infrastructure investments in support of EU water directives compliance, and support water service development strategy implementation.Phase 1 is organized into four components:Component 1 — Strategic Investments in Water Services Infrastructure and Resilience aims to rehabilitate, modernize, and expand water services infrastructure across priority municipalities. Activities focus on: rehabilitation and expansion of centralized water supply systems to reduce water losses, construction of sewer networks connecting households to existing wastewater treatment plants; and modernization of decentralized water services infrastructure to serve rural populations in remote areas.Component 2 — Integrated Approaches for Coastal Water Security addresses the growing risk to coastal water supply posed by the declining discharge of the Bolje Sestre spring, linked to extensive gravel extraction from the Morača River. It includes: expansion of Lake Skadar water abstraction and treatment capacity by 250 liters per second to address near-term supply shortfalls during peak summer seasons; and comprehensive hydromorphological and sediment assessments, monitoring, and modeling of the Morača River to identify and design a long-term solution for spring discharge stabilization, to be implemented under Phase 2.Component 3 — Strengthening the Enabling Environment for Effective Water Sector Governance and Management finances the policy, regulatory, and capacity reforms essential to making water sector investments sustainable and effective. Activities are organized around three clusters: policy and legislative support, including the Techno-Economic Analysis and National Strategy for Water Services Development mandated by the new Law on Water Services; regulatory support, including revision of REGAGEN's tariff methodology and development of a national digital platform for utility performance data collection and benchmarking; and capacity building for utilities and local governments focused on multi-annual business planning, NRW management, and performance data reporting.Component 4 — Project Management supports effective coordination and oversight of all project activities through the PIU within PROCON, with fiduciary functions handled by the Technical Services Unit within the Ministry of Finance.

P513113.project_abstract: The water supply and sanitation services in The Gambia are currently facing a significant crisis due to ongoing challenges in achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). According to the 2023 Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP) report, only 48% of the population has access to safely managed water services, with 68% access in urban areas compared to just 12% in rural areas. Access to basic sanitation services is also a major concern, as only 28% of the population has access to safely managed sanitation, while 15% still practice open defecation, particularly in rural regions. Additionally, the country lacks adequate wastewater treatment, with about 80% of wastewater improperly managed, leading to groundwater contamination and posing significant public health risks. Three key localities in the Greater Banjul Area (GBA)—Banjul, Kanifing, and Brikama—are home to approximately 63% of the country's population. The National Water and Electricity Company (NAWEC) currently produces 139,000 cubic meters of water daily, which is insufficient to meet the demand. The GBA mainly relies on boreholes that draw water from a sub-shallow aquifer; however, this water source is vulnerable to contamination from untreated sewage and saltwater intrusion caused by rising sea levels related to climate change. The Gambia Water and Sanitation Project (WASAP) aims to support the government's efforts to address the crisis in water and sanitation services in the Greater Banjul Area (GBA) region. This operation includes a series of activities organized into four mutually reinforcing components, which are summarized below: Component 1: Improving Access to Urban Drinking Water Services (US$14 million)             Component 2: Improving Access to Sanitation Services ($18.0 million)Component 2.1: Urban Sanitation (16.5 million)   • Emergency wastewater management ($5.0 million)   • Expansion of Kotu Wastewater System (US$3.2 million)   • Construction of a Fecal Sludge Treatment Plant ($8.3 million)Component 2.2: Rural Sanitation Development ($1.5 million)   • Construction of Safely Managed Latrines in Targeted Rural Areas ($0.9 million)   • Development of a National Sanitation Master Plan (US$0.6 M)Component 3. Institutional Reform and Capacity Building for Sustainable Water Services ($6.0 million)   • Support to sector reform and NAWEC operational and financial efficacy improvement ($3.0 million)   • Supporting transboundary cooperation on the Senegal-Mauritanian Aquifer Basin ($0.5 million)   • Enhancing water resources monitoring and conservation ($2.5 million)Component 4. Project management: ($2 million)   Component 5. Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC)   • Component 5 	CERC 	$0 millionTOTAL: 	$30 million

P513301.project_abstract: The Project aims to increase access to drinking water supply and sanitation (WSS) services in underserved areas in the city of Kinshasa, the capital city of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). The project intends to leverage financing from IDA, as well as mobilize private capital for water supply production. The aim is to provide safely managed drinking water services to previously unserved populations and to improve the quality of services from basic to safely managed drinking water services in Kinshasa through safe and reliable water access through household connections. Moreover, the project will improve access to safely-managed sanitation through the construction of fecal sludge treatment plants (FSTP), and increasing access to water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) facilities in selected schools and health centers within the city. Aligned with the regional Multi-phase Programmatic Approach (MPA) design, the proposed components are the following: Component 1: Strengthening Governance and Reforms and Improving the Performance of Service Providers. Subcomponent 1.1. on Governance and Reforms (mapped to pillars 1 and 2 of the MPA) finances technical assistance, training and equipment to address governance bottlenecks and support the implementation of reform measures including for the mobilization of private capital for WASH. This includes the development of regulatory instruments and policies, tariff studies, transaction advisory services, capacity development for sector ministries, and the national water and sanitation regulatory entity. Subcomponent 1.2. on Service Providers Performance Improvement, which is mapped to pillar 3 of the MPA, finances works, equipment, technical assistance, training for the national water utility REGIDESO. This covers support for the implementation of REGIDESO's Performance Improvement Action Plan, including Non-Revenue Water, digitalization, and water quality management improvements and social connection subsidies. Component 2: Leveraging Financing for Resilient Investments to Expand WASH Access (mapped to pillar 4 of the MPA). Subcomponent 2.1 finances infrastructure investments in climate -resilient urban water production and distribution systems for Kinshasa, including viability gap financing for the construction of new bulk water treatment plant(s). A Build-Operate-Transfer (BOT) arrangement is proposed for these water production plant(s), blending public and private funding to ensure an affordable tariff for REGIDESO as the off-taker, and to the households as end-consumers. IDA financing would support the viability gap financing, as well as the completion of the rehabilitation and expansion of the existing Ndjili water treatment plant, and expansion of REGIDESO’s water distribution network to maximize impact on the population. MIGA de-risking instruments will be brought as appropriate. The IFC role would be to provide debt and mobilize other lenders (either through co-development with value-for-money assurances or through a competitive process). Subcomponent 2.2 finances construction of one or more fecal sludge treatment plants (FSTPs) in Kinshasa, as well as technical assistance and support to strengthen the liquid sanitation collection, conveyance, treatment and reuse chain. This component will also provide support for climate-resilient WASH facilities in schools and health centers. Component 3: Project Management and Coordination. This component finances the overall project management, monitoring and coordination costs, including operating costs. It also finances costs related to land acquisition and resettlement related to the project. The total project cost estimation is US$500 million, of which US$200 million to be financed by IDA, US$200 million by other development partners and US$100 million in private capital mobilization.

P513389.project_abstract: The proposed project aims to foster sustainable tourism in selected destination supporting the creation of local jobs, prioritizing environmental stewardship, and strengthening community resilience. By developing unique tourism offerings that respect natural assets and empower local communities, the initiative seeks to drive inclusive economic growth, thereby creating conditions for sustained private sector participation.

P513708.project_abstract: Ghana has a road network of about 94,203 km, of which 27 percent paved and 73% unpaved. More than 50 percent of the network, including many feeder and secondary roads, is in fair to impassable condition. Road density and coverage are unevenly distributed: the north of the country remains poorly served, while the southern half has greater road density supporting agricultural and livestock production, but often at a poor service level that increases travel distances and costs to markets and limits opportunities. In fact, the population in the north is better connected to neighbor’s countries and has some comparative productivity agricultural advantages. To address these challenges, the Government of Ghana launched the Big Push Initiative in 2025. The Program is aimed at reducing the investment gap in key sectors and revitalizing aging, poorly maintained infrastructure. Among its priorities are feeder roads, which play a crucial role in linking rural areas to main transport corridors. Investing in feeder roads is essential to connect farms and rural communities with markets, reduce transport costs and post-harvest losses, and improve access to services. Strengthening these networks will boost agricultural productivity, competitiveness, and inclusive growth, while better connecting isolated regions of the country. The proposed operation will support (i) the rehabilitation of selected feeder roads linked to key agricultural value chains, (ii) the maintenance of the rehabilitated roads and Road Maintenance Trust Fund reform operationalization, and (iii) project management and OHS enhancement. The proposed operation will be structured around 3 components: Component 1: Feeder road rehabilitation Component 2: Road maintenance and support to Road Maintenance Trut Fund (RMTF) Component 3: Project management and implementation supportComponent 4: CERC

P514166.project_abstract: The project will build on investments being made under the current water Project for resilience and water security for Comoros. It will expand the current scope  and include both water and sanitation investments, and geographically expand beyond the capital city of Moroni to  include investments in Anjouan and Moheli islands. Building on the pillars of the parent MPA, the project will have a deliberate focus on leveraging regional knowledge and innovation to enhance sector reforms, governance and service delivery. The project will also support preparatory activities for potential interventions on solid waste management, which negatively impacts water quality, human health and  tourism, all of which are critical elements for prosperity.

P514199.project_abstract: The project will be the first operation of an MPA, conceived to allow scalability of World Bank support for local development in rural northern Mozambique. The program design envisages two simultaneous phases implemented over an eight-year period. The phases will address three key development needs of rural northern Mozambique communities that are impacted by fragility: social cohesion, access to economic opportunities, and access to resilient infrastructure. These will be achieved by revitalizing forums for dialogue between the northern provinces, within communities, and between communities and the state, supporting a cash transfer program for vulnerable households, supporting youth upskilling and SME development, providing GBV and psycho-social support services, building systems for community participation in local development, enhancing the capacity of local government for better planning and efficient service delivery, and investing in priority community and public infrastructure. Both phases will be implemented in the three northern provinces of Cabo Delgado, Nampula, and Niassa. This US$100 million IDA Phase I operation will develop and remobilize foundational regional, province, and district-level dialogue forums, while local economic development activities and community infrastructure and basic service planning activities and investments will concentrate in selected 80 localities across the provinces’ 56 districts and public infrastructure will be concentrated in 10 districts of Cabo Delgado province. A US$150 million IDA Phase II operation will deepen local development systems and capacities and make sure they are sustainable while developing community and public infrastructure and services in additional localities according to readiness criteria.

P514340.project_abstract: Support to the potable water sector in Tunisia.

P514502.project_abstract: Since 2017, the World Bank has supported Yemen’s health sector through large-scale emergency operations that have delivered essential health, nutrition, and water, sanitation and hygiene (WASH) services to millions, while gradually investing in local institutions and systems. These efforts have sustained human capital in a protracted crisis, reaching nearly 24 million beneficiaries and training over 18,000 health personnel. Despite achievements, challenges in Yemen persist—including low immunization rates, malnutrition, conflict-related access constraints, declining donor funding, and reliance on external third-party implementation. The upcoming operation will prioritize integration, strengthen local capacity, and revamp health workforce development by aligning community cadres with national strategies, improving training systems, and creating job opportunities for health workers.  The project will consist of 5 components: Component 1: Delivery of integrated health, nutrition and WASH servicesComponent 1 will prioritize delivering an integrated minimum service package (MSP) across all levels of care—primary, secondary, and tertiary. Investments will be made to improve water and sanitation infrastructure at primary health centers. A Geographic Accelerator sub-component will tailor area-based interventions to local needs, combining MSP delivery with nutrition, WASH, and social protection interventions.Component 2: Support to prevent and respond to communicable disease outbreaksComponent 2 will retain emergency response capacity through disease surveillance, rapid response teams, laboratory support. It will also deliver emergency water trucking and to hospitals and support water quality testing. Component 3: Systems building for sustainable delivery of health and nutrition servicesComponent 3 focuses on building sustainable systems for health and nutrition service delivery by strengthening sub-national institutions and the health workforce. It includes tailored training for ministries and local health offices in planning, monitoring, and financial management, alongside support for refining national health strategies. A dedicated subcomponent will address critical health workforce reforms, aiming to resolve systemic bottlenecks and establish more sustainable funding mechanisms for health worker salaries. These reforms will also contribute to job creation and retention in the health sector, helping transition from volunteer-based service delivery to a more stable and professionalized workforce.Component 4: Project managementComponent 4 will consist of supervision costs, monitoring and evaluation, third party monitoring and financing of E&S compliance requirements, fiduciary aspects and procurement. Component 5: Contingent emergency response category (CERC)A zero cost CERC component will support the operation in the case of emergencies.
P514502.regionname: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan, and Pakistan

P515014.project_abstract: The first phase of Clean Philippines Multiphase Programmatic Approach will cover Metro Manila, the National Capital Region (NCR), including all of its 17 LGUs.  The project consists of four components: Component 1. Strengthening policy and institutions for improved solid waste management, Component 2. Improving local service delivery for better solid waste management, Component 3. Improving waste infrastructure and plastic waste management for a circular economy, and Component 4. Innovation, learning, and public awareness for behavioral change.  This first phase will be led by Metro Manila Development Authority (MMDA) and will be supported by the Department of Environment and Natural Resources (DENR) and the Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG). This phase addresses one of the country’s SWM hotspots, in an area where LGU capacity is among the highest nationwide. The project will improve solid waste management and reduce plastic pollution in the National Capital Region of the Philippines.
